title: Writing - Part 1: Nuclear Energy
description: Your class has listened to a lecture on nuclear energy and the ongoing debate about its role in addressing climate change and securing long-term energy supply.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the considerations in your notes. You should explain which consideration you regard as most significant when evaluating the future of nuclear energy, giving reasons for your opinion.

considerations: The potential of nuclear power to provide large-scale, low-carbon electricity generation
Safety risks and the long-term management of radioactive waste
The economic costs and lengthy construction timelines of nuclear power plants
opinions: Nuclear power stations emit negligible greenhouse gases during operation and can generate electricity continuously regardless of weather conditions, making them a potentially valuable complement to intermittent renewable sources such as wind and solar in a decarbonised energy system.

High-profile accidents at Three Mile Island, Chernobyl and Fukushima have demonstrated that nuclear failures can have catastrophic and long-lasting environmental consequences, and no country has yet implemented a permanent geological repository for the high-level radioactive waste that nuclear plants continue to accumulate.

New nuclear projects in Western Europe and North America have consistently exceeded their budgets and timelines by substantial margins, raising questions about whether the capital and political will required could be more efficiently directed towards scaling up renewable technologies and grid-level energy storage.

structure.introduction: Introduce the debate around nuclear energy's role in the energy transition, identifying the two considerations your essay will examine.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Analyse the first consideration with specific examples, explaining its implications for energy and climate policy.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the second consideration and argue why it represents the most significant factor in evaluating nuclear energy's future.
structure.conclusion: Summarise your argument and offer a brief reflection on the conditions under which nuclear energy could play a constructive role.

content.modelAnswer:
The question of whether nuclear energy should form part of the response to climate change has become one of the most contested issues in contemporary energy policy. Proponents point to its low-carbon credentials and reliability, while critics highlight profound risks and structural economic weaknesses. Among the most important considerations are its capacity for large-scale low-carbon generation and the unresolved challenge of radioactive waste management.

Nuclear power's ability to generate substantial quantities of electricity without emitting greenhouse gases during operation represents a genuine asset in decarbonisation strategies. Unlike wind and solar, which depend on weather conditions, nuclear plants can provide stable baseload power that supports grid reliability as fossil fuels are phased out. France's decades-long reliance on nuclear generation — sustaining among the lowest carbon intensities of any major economy — demonstrates that this potential is realisable at scale.

Nevertheless, I would argue that the unresolved problem of radioactive waste constitutes the most significant consideration, because it represents an obligation extending across geological timescales. Despite decades of nuclear operation worldwide, no permanent repository for high-level waste has been commissioned. Finland's Onkalo facility remains the only project approaching operational status. Leaving future generations to manage materials that remain hazardous for tens of thousands of years raises profound ethical questions that economic or climate arguments cannot simply dissolve.

In conclusion, nuclear energy may contribute to near-term decarbonisation, but a credible expansion of the sector requires, as a minimum condition, a demonstrated and fully funded solution to the waste legacy it creates.
